title: High-dimensional neuronal activity from low-dimensional latent dynamics: a solvable model

reviews.summary: This paper bridges the apparent tension between low-dimensional latent dynamics and high-dimensional recorded neural activity. First, the authors construct an analytically solvable low-rank RNN on a ring in which the pre-activation dynamics reduce exactly to a 2D system exhibiting a limit cycle, yet the post-activation activity has a heavy-tailed covariance spectrum with a power-law decay (α = 2). They then generalize via random feature kernel theory and conjecture a simple relationship between the eigenspectrum tail exponent, latent (pre-activation) dimension, and activation smoothness, and develop a new latent variable method (Neural Cross-Encoder, NCE) to infer pre-activation dimensionality from large-scale two-photon data. Empirically, they find that drifting gratings and spontaneous activity are well explained by low-dimensional pre-activations, whereas natural images require high-dimensional pre-activations.
strengths: Exact, analytically solvable low-rank RNN whose pre-activation dynamics reduce to a 2D effective system with a closed-form vector field; the model yields a post-activation covariance given by a known arc-cosine kernel with an exact α = 2 power law (clear, elegant connection).
Sharp conceptual distinction between pre- and post-activation linear dimensions, clarifying how low-dimensional dynamical systems can generate high-dimensional recorded activity.

A simple, testable conjecture linking the eigenspectrum tail exponent to latent dimension and activation smoothness (α = 1 + (2p+1)/(d−1)) that unifies known special cases and matches simulations over a broad range of parameters.

Neural Cross-Encoder (NCE): an interpretable latent model with a single-layer monotone decoder that directly targets pre-activation dimensionality and uses cross-encoding to remove unshared variability, extending Reduced Rank Regression in a principled nonlinear manner.
Large-scale volumetric two-photon datasets (tens of thousands of neurons), carefully analyzed across three conditions (drifting gratings, natural images, spontaneous).

Methodological care to use different stimulus repeats for source/target splits to remove shared non-stimulus variability in the stimulus-driven analyses; held-out evaluation; multiple sessions; simulation sanity checks where NCE recovers ground-truth latents.
Consistent dimensionality estimates across sessions and conditions (gratings ~5–6, spontaneous ~7, natural images ~94 for the NCE model at 95% explainable variance).

The pre- vs post-activation dimensionality framing is well-motivated and consistently used; derivations are well signposted and supported by appendices; figures are clear and well-connected to the text.
Explanations of how random feature kernels arise and how they relate to eigenspectra and latent dimension are pedagogical and compelling.
Provides a principled reconciliation of low-dimensional latent dynamics with high-dimensional activity—a timely contribution given ongoing debates sparked by large-scale calcium imaging studies.
Offers a practical tool (NCE) to infer pre-activation dimensionality from data, enabling more interpretable characterization of latent structure and its dependence on stimulus conditions.
weaknesses: The solvable RNN relies on strong idealizations (Heaviside nonlinearity, cosine ring connectivity, exact rank-2 structure, infinite-width/time limits). While instructive, the robustness of the exact α = 2 result under more biologically realistic nonlinearities and connectivity is not demonstrated analytically.

The central spectral statement for general settings (Conjecture 1) is unproven; while well-motivated and numerically supported, the lack of even partial theoretical guarantees in new regimes (e.g., non-integer p, nonzero bias c, finite-sample rates) limits the formal strength of claims.

Identifiability of the learned latents in NCE is only up to invertible linear transforms; while sufficient for dimensionality, the paper could be clearer about the implications for interpreting learned factors.

Baseline comparisons for NCE are limited to a linear model (Reduced Rank Regression). Comparisons to established nonlinear latent models (e.g., LFADS/sequential VAEs, pi-VAE, deep switching dynamical systems, MARBLE) are missing, leaving unclear whether NCE’s dimensionality estimates are specific to its constrained decoder or generally consistent.

Natural image condition uses only two repeats per image, which may be underpowered for cross-trial denoising and could bias dimensionality estimates upward due to low SNR and harder optimization; the paper acknowledges training challenges but does not provide systematic robustness analyses (e.g., vs. number of repeats, data size, or regularization).

Analyses are restricted to subsets of neurons (e.g., 1000 targets, top responsive), which may introduce selection bias; sensitivity to the size/composition of source/target sets and neuron selection is not explored.
Limited ablations: effect of encoder depth/width, activation family, learned p and c distributions, and source/target split ratios on estimated dimensionality are not reported.

The paper’s use of “heavy tail” could be clarified: stating “decays strictly faster than 1/n” is unusual since “heavy-tailed” typically denotes slower-than-exponential decay, and, in some neural literature, α near 1 is emphasized. A consistent definition and rationale for the threshold would help.

Some derivations crucial to intuition (e.g., conditions yielding Eq. (5), properties of the correlation kernel on the circle) are deferred to the appendix; a short sketch in main text would strengthen accessibility.

Missing or light connections to cross-validated latent factor approaches and cross-trial denoising strategies used in neuroscience (e.g., dPCA variants, cross-validated PCA/factor analysis, stimulus-locked GLMs with latent structure).
Additional discussion of eigenvalue decay results for dot-product kernels on spheres (beyond the citations) and how the conjecture aligns/conflicts with sharp results would further anchor the theory.
reviews.questions:
1. Can you provide any partial theoretical progress toward Conjecture 1 (e.g., special cases with non-integer p, nonzero bias c, or new bounds that recover the proposed exponent), or formalize conditions under which the exponent is guaranteed?
2. How sensitive are NCE’s dimensionality estimates to encoder architecture (depth/width), the choice of activation family in the decoder (e.g., other monotone functions), and regularization? Please provide variance across seeds/hyperparameters.
3. For the natural image condition with only two repeats, can you quantify how dimensionality estimates change with synthetic repeat augmentation, subsetting images, or bootstrapping trials? Do you have sessions with more repeats to validate robustness?
4. How does the choice of source/target split (e.g., 500/1000 vs other ratios, random vs stratified selection) and neuron selection criteria (e.g., responsiveness threshold) affect dimensionality estimates?
5. Could you compare NCE to at least one established nonlinear latent model (e.g., LFADS/pi-VAE/MARBLE) in terms of explainable variance and inferred dimensionality on the same datasets, even if the interpretability of latents differs?
6. In the solvable RNN, does the α = 2 result persist (empirically or analytically) under smoother nonlinearities (e.g., ReLU or softplus) and/or small deviations from the cosine ring weights (e.g., additional Fourier components, weak randomness)?
7. Can you decompose NCE-predicted spontaneous activity into components explained by measured behavior (running speed, pupil, etc.) to clarify how much of the low-dimensional pre-activation structure is behaviorally driven?
8. Is code for NCE and for reproducing the theoretical/numerical kernel experiments planned to be released? Providing this would greatly enhance reproducibility and adoption.
reviews.assessment:
This paper makes a compelling theoretical and empirical contribution to an important ongoing discussion: how to reconcile low-dimensional latent dynamics with high-dimensional activity observed in large-scale recordings. The solvable RNN example is elegant and precise, the kernel-theoretic perspective is insightful, and the Neural Cross-Encoder offers a clearly interpretable way to estimate pre-activation dimensionality from data. The empirical findings—that gratings and spontaneous activity are low-dimensional in pre-activation space while natural images are not—are intriguing and align with intuition, though the latter claim is tempered by limited repeats and acknowledged optimization challenges.

The main reservations concern (i) the central conjecture being unproven (even partial new theory would substantially strengthen the work), and (ii) limited empirical baselines for NCE against other nonlinear latent models, as well as incomplete robustness analyses (repeats, splits, neuron selection, hyperparameters). Despite these issues, the paper’s conceptual clarity, analytical results in the solvable case, and the practical utility of NCE make it a valuable contribution for the NeurIPS community. I lean toward acceptance, contingent on clarifying the above points and, ideally, adding at least a comparative baseline and robustness analyses.
